segment-text: EXTREMELY WICKED, SHOCKINGLY EVIL, AND VILE
based on true events
Numbered Pre-Production Draft (12/11/17)
THE BUZZSAW POWERCHORDS OF THE RAMONES (OR SOMETHING SIMILAR) CARRY US THROUGH FAST CUT ARCHIVAL FOOTAGE OF HOW THE WORLD WAS IN THE '70S, BEFORE IT KNEW TED BUNDY...
Ho!
Let's go! Let's go!

It was sexy... BELL BOTTOMS... FLARES... SUNDRESSES... FARRAH FAWCET... DAVID BOWIE... etc. It was cool... MUSCLE CARS... PUNK ROCK... STEVE MCQUEEN... IGGY POP... etc. It was eerily similar to the world today... WATERGATE... VIETNAM... PROTESTS... RACIAL TENSION... TERRORISM... etc. Amid these iconic images, we INTERCUT 8MM/16MM FOOTAGE of all the young people at Seattle's LAKE SAMMAMISH on July 14, 1974... sunbathing... swimming... laughing... having fun... A more innocent time back then. Evil still looked like "evil" and it was easily recognizable... MANSON... ZODIAC... SON OF SAM... GACY... and therefore it was easily avoidable.
ABSTRACT SHOTS OF... female hands opening an envelope... unfolding a long, multi-page letter... eyes reading...
This will be my last letter and if anyone deserves to hear it as it was, it's you. Gradually, the innocent scenes from Lake Sam give rise to

NEWS REPORTS of the disappearance of two young women -Janice Ott and Denise Naslund -- within an hour of each other, at busy Lake Sam, in the middle of broad daylight. INTERCUT SHOTS OF... police interviewing beachgoers... detectives canvassing the area... a sketch artist at work...
After I clear my name, which will undoubtedly require a modicum of legal wrangling, I intend to get my hands on whoever's responsible for these unconscionable crimes to ensure they never do it again.
Meanwhile, the Ramones start to slow... the vocals get deeper... the song sounds WARPED...
I admit, I am in a little trouble, but it's all going to work out.
INTERCUT AUDIO of witnesses giving generic, if not completely
He had blond hair / He had brown hair / He was British / He was American / He drove a light car / He drove a dark car /
CROSSFADING DOWN as a dangerous SCORE FADES UP...
If anything goes wrong, you'll be reading about me in the papers.
A NEWSPAPER THUDS DOWN INTO FRAME -- with a generic looking POLICE SKETCH OF A MAN. Could be anyone. Your neighbor.
OVER, WE HEAR ONE SIDE OF A PHONE CALL (Shoot it too):

King County Sheriff's Department. (beat) Whoa, whoa, whoa, can you take a deep breath, ma'am, slow down, and describe him for me? (beat) Does he look anything like the sketch? (beat) A minor resemblance is still a resemblance, I guess. What kind of
car does he drive?
CRESCENDOS to an unpleasant volume, churning a sense of dread
SMASH TO BLACK.
Your love gives me hope. has. It always will.
It always
After a long beat... the safe, soft, saccharine melody of the Archies' 1969 hit, "Sugar, Sugar", kicks in...
Can't believe the loveliness of loving you... (backup vocals) Can't believe it's true...
INT. SANDPIPER TAVERN - NIGHT
We're taken back to...
Five Years Earlier The height of innocence and bubblegum pop. It's ladies night
at a popular college bar. Two frothy beers plunk down on a table crowded with empties, where LIZ commiserates with her best friend JOANNA.
Why did you have to drag me to a college bar? I feel invisible here.
The best way to get over someone is to get under someone.
No guy wants to go with a single mom who works as a secretary.
Tonight you're not a single mom, you're single. You're not a secretary, you have a secretary. And how can you be invisible if that guy's been staring at you all
night?
Liz glances across the way at someone who causes her to momentarily lose herself. We don't see him yet -- we stay on her. Because she just fell in love.
You are my candy girl... And you got me wanting you...
Liz looks away. Takes a long pull on her beer.
He's not staring at me, he's
staring at the blonde on the dance floor who's not wearing a bra.
I think he's coming over.
I think if I hear one more love song I'm going to puke.

Liz gets up. Stumbles. She's had a few. On her way to the jukebox, she fumbles coins from her pocket which roll away. As she curses to herself and crouches to find them, TWO FEET step up. The stranger opens his palm revealing a single coin. Liz rises to meet TED. Handsome. Dapper. A smile that turns strangers into friends. She has yet to look away from his beautiful blue eyes. We have too. He has a clean cut, preppy look compared to all the longhairs.
It's my last dime so we better make it a good one.
In the background, Joanna checks out his ass. emphatic approval. Liz can't find her voice. Gives Liz
I haven't seen you here before.
It's the first time I've been out since I moved to Seattle.
Explains the new car smell.
No, that's just Utah.
That's in Wyoming, right?
Liz hides a smile. They flip through the selection cards.
So what do you do? Race car driver?
School?
Liz glances at Joanna in the background. Tries out her lie:
I make heart valves.
I'm a lawyer. dire? Ever hear of voir
Is that Latin or are you slurring too?
Ted bashfully laughs -- one of his endearing qualities.
Is it that obvious?
So what are you going to play for me, counselor?
He pretends like he's studying her.

Given it's taken you this long to leave your seat, I'd say you're probably ready to move. Since you needed four beers to get there, I suspect you're usually too shy to dance. But you left it up to me, so you're willing to take a risk. And seeing as you spent the entire night crying to your girlfriend instead of noticing me noticing you, I'm guessing probably not a love song. Unless you're feeling hopeful? This guy's good.
How are you feeling?
Ted inserts the coin. Punches a number.
Like we could both use some company.
The opening pep of "Build Me Up Buttercup" by the Foundations rile up the place. Liz wilts in shyness. Ted pulls her toward the dance floor.
What's the matter? They don't have dancing in Utah? What do they call folks from Utah anyway? Utonians? Utahites? Utahcrats?
Liz considers him. His eyes. His ease. His ear-to-ear grin that's as goofy as it is charming. There's something about him that's magnetic. Liz downs the rest of her beer.
(hick voice) Utaaaaaaahns.
They laugh and start dancing with complete and utter reckless abandon. With the complete innocence of youth.
Why do you build me up...? Buttercup, baby, just to let me down... And mess me around... And then worst of all...
After a while, Ted pulls Liz close. Feels more intimate:
What do you say we get outta here?
I don't even know your name.
But you already feel like you've known me all your life.
How do you know that?
Because I feel the same. The electricity is undeniable.
leans in to kiss her... Wait. Each sizzling with desire, he
I don't make heart valves.
(shrugs) I'm not a lawyer. Then, as if the world disappears around them, they kiss. THE FOUNDATIONS (V.O.) Buttercup, don't break my heart...
EXT. SANDPIPER TAVERN - NIGHT
They stumble out laughing, heading for Liz's car.
I'm applying to law school after I graduate -- if I ever finish my Chinese Studies major, that is.
Does that mean you speak Chinese?
Wo zai sho-hua.
What did you just say?
Ted shrugs. They both laugh. misses the keyhole on her car. Liz, clearly the drunker one,

(John Wayne impression) Now, listen here, pilgrim. A cowboy never lets a lassie drive drunk. Life is hard, but it's harder if you're stupid, and gettin' behind that wheel is ri-goddamn-diculous. So hand 'em here, or I'm gonna hafta frisk ya'.
She teasingly hides her keys behind her back so he has to put his arms around her to get them.
EXT. UPSCALE NEIGHBORHOOD - NIGHT
Ted's VW BUG pulls over in a nice neighborhood.
Why are we stopping?
I wanna show you something.
HOLD ON the IDLING ENGINE of Ted's Bug. Ted and Liz stroll arm-in-arm through the upscale neighborhood, brown-bagging a bottle of wine, his coat around her, comfortably close now.
Someday, when I'm a big-wig lawyer, I'm going to buy one of these houses.
There's one I like, it's big, with a brick chimney, and a view of the Puget Sound, not far from here.
I can see it already -- warm fires on cold Seattle nights, barbeques in the backyard with friends...
And a Mercedes in the driveway?
We should get a dog too! A big one that we can take on hikes with us. In fact, I can hear him barking now. Do you hear it?
He makes her listen to the peaceful night breeze -- then BARKS really loud in her ear, scaring her half to death. They laugh and cuddle, the stars infinite and bright above.
INT. LIZ'S HOUSE - NIGHT
They arrive at her front door. Ted waits expectantly but Liz looks hesitant about inviting him in.
Guess I better get going.
His disappointment is palpable. Liz is obviously conflicted. Then the front door OPENS, revealing a HIGH SCHOOL GIRL with her coat and backpack. Liz blushes, embarrassed.
Sorry to interrupt, but you said you'd be back two hours ago and I have school tomorrow.
Ted, this is Rachel. My sitter. (then) I understand if you want to go.
Liz braces for rejection like she's used to it.
Why on earth would I want to do that?
Liz sighs with relief.
INT. LIZ'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
Ted and Liz look in on her BABY, asleep in a crib.
Are you sure you don't want to run?
Only if it's after you.
She smiles. They lay down on the bed, fully clothed. She puts her head on his chest and closes her eyes. They fall asleep together. Tender, sweet, and innocent.
INT. LIZ'S BEDROOM - DAY

Sunlight floods the room. Liz is sprawled out in bed. As she wakes, the night's events start to come back to her. She looks over at the other side of the bed -- it's empty. And so is the crib. Liz dashes out in a panic-8
INT. KITCHEN - DAY

--as Ted turns around with a knife, chopping omelette veggies, cooing at Liz's baby in a high chair. An idyllic, domestic scene with brilliant morning light saturating everything. He flashes that smile again when he notices Liz.
You looked so pretty sleeping, I

didn't want to-She cuts him off with a kiss. This is what love looks like. SMASH TO: EXTREMELY WICKED, SHOCKINGLY EVIL, AND VILE BEGIN TITLE SEQUENCE: A collage of PHOTOS and HOME MOVIES that show the blossoming of a spectacular relationship: Two beautiful young lovers... a baby that's growing up... holidays... vacations... birthdays... ticket stubs... a love we all hope for. The images start to come faster, the passage of time shown by the baby growing into a LITTLE GIRL, faster, faster still... SMASH TO:
EXT. SUBURBAN STREET - NIGHT
A Volkswagen Bug sputters by dark houses in the pre-dawn hour. SUPER: Utah.
INT. VOLKSWAGEN BUG - CONTINUOUS

Ted's hunched over the wheel, driving the quiet, dark street. His hair is longer, shaggier now. In his rearview, he notices another car tailing him, which he can only make as a silhouette behind the headlights. Ted gives it a little gas but the other car keeps pace. Suddenly the high beams flip on. He squints from the reflection in his rearview. Scared now, Ted punches it.
EXT. SUBURBAN STREET - CONTINUOUS
The Volkswagen Bug accelerates. Ted burns through a stop sign. The pursuing car keeps pace. Only then do we realize that the pursuing car is a cop!
INT. VOLKSWAGEN BUG - CONTINUOUS
Red and blue lights play over Ted's face. steers over.
EXT. SUBURBAN STREET - CONTINUOUS
He curses and Ted cuts the engine and gets out. He strolls to the rear of his car as the cop pulls in behind. Ted shrugs, flashes that million dollar smile -- now becoming familiar to us.
(sheepishly) I guess I'm lost.
The cop is BOB HAYWARD, a no-nonsense highway patrol veteran on the tail end of his shift. Smiles don't work on him.
You ran two stop signs, son.
I do apologize, Officer, but I couldn't make your car with the headlights in my eyes and, well, I guess I got spooked.
Got a license?
Ted produces his ID.
What's a kid from Seattle doing in Granger, Utah this time of morning?
I caught a late movie at the Redwood but I obviously missed the interstate on my way home. These subdivisions, man...
Must have been a good movie, drove all the way from Washington.
I live in Salt Lake now. I'm a law student at the University of Utah.
Hayward pans his flashlight inside Ted's car.

(small talk) My girlfriend lives in Seattle. I'm going to propose once I graduate. Right now, I'm just trying to save money for a ring so I'd really appreciate getting off without a ticket, if you can find the heart.
What'd you see tonight?
The Towering Inferno.
Hayward pans the flashlight back on Ted. SMASH TO: HANDCUFFS snap around Ted's wrists. Hayward shuts Ted in the back of a squad car.
INT. INTERROGATION ROOM - NIGHT
Ted laughs, shaking his head at the absurdity of events as Hayward removes items from a black canvas duffle bag...
It's just junk I picked up around the house, I swear.
Garbage bags... flashlight... gloves... rope... (NOTE: Additional items need to be shot; see end montage.) "POSSESSION OF BURGLARY TOOLS," reads the arrest report.
Ever been to the town of Murray?
INT. LINE UP ROOM
Ted and seven doppelgangers file into a lineup facing their own reflections in a two-way mirror. Ted steps forward, turns profile, swallows hard.
INT. PHOTO ROOM
Ted turns for a mug shot, eyes ringed with shock. JUDGE HANSON (PRE-LAP)
The flash bulb POPS.
INT. DISTRICT COURT - ARRAIGNMENT HEARING - DAY
Ted stands for arraignment, face drained of color.

Trial is set for the twenty-third of February. (MORE) JUDGE HANSON (O.S.) (CONT'D) You shall remain in the custody of the Salt Lake County Sheriff's Department. Bail is set at one hundred thousand dollars. The GAVEL-POUNDS.
INT. UNIVERSITY MEDICAL DIVISION - DAY
Liz types at her desk. A FRAMED PICTURE of her and Ted caught in a moment of uncontrollable laughter beside.
Hey Liz, isn't this your boyfriend?

Liz's work neighbor JERRY -- unassuming, kinda shlubby -leans over to show her a newspaper. What she sees stuns her. She starts to feel uncomfortable when she notices coworkers looking at her. (She's the only woman who works here.)
INT. INTERVIEW ROOM - UTAH - DAY
Ted's lawyer JOHN O'CONNELL enters in his usual cowboy hat and boots. He's tall, bearded, with overgrown black hair.
John, what the hell is going on?
Let's start on November 8th, 1974.

If I can't remember where I was on a random night one year ago, it's because my memory does not improve with time. It's safe to say what I was not doing, however. I was not having heart surgery, nor was I taking ballet lessons, nor was I in Mexico, nor was I abducting a complete stranger.
An alibi would be the best first step.
I don't even know this woman! My heart goes out to her because that is a truly awful thing for anyone to have gone through, but she's obviously mistaking me for someone else.
The good news is there's no physical evidence and eye witness testimony is precarious at best.
Why is this happening?
You remember anything about two girls disappearing at a lake last year in Seattle?
(searching) Yeah, I remember a police sketch of the suspect in the paper -- all my friends teased me about a resemblance. No one was serious, though.
Someone must have been because your name landed on a list. When the Utah police got wind of that, they started looking at you with more serious eyes because a similar thing has been happening here.
I have a girlfriend, who has a young daughter, both of whom I love dearly. I would never hurt a woman.

There are twelve hundred names on that list and last I checked, brown hair and blue eyes are hardly grounds for conviction. It's an open and shut case, as far as I'm concerned. Let me do what I do best and in the end it'll be nothing more than a funny story you tell at cocktail parties. You're posting tomorrow.
My bail's a hundred grand.
For a boyscout with no priors? knocked it down to fifteen.
I don't have fifteen.
Your pals in the Washington State governor's office passed a hat around. You think Utah's top defense attorney works pro bono? Ted smiles.
POURING RAIN (PRE-LAP)
INT. KITCHEN - LIZ'S HOUSE - DAY

Wine POURS into a glass. Liz anxiously bounces her knee at the table with the newspaper Jerry showed her laid out before her. She takes a big drink. Also at the table, her daughter MOLLY (now 8) draws a picture of a shark with crayons. The DOORBELL RINGS. Before Molly gets up-Stay here.
INT. FOYER - DAY
Liz opens the door, revealing Ted holding a newspaper over his head in the rain. As he takes her face in his hands to kiss her -- she slaps him.
Jesus, Liz! What was that for?
Liz shows him the newspaper he was using as an umbrella. He's mortified by the headline.
How many stop signs did you run?
INT. LIVING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
We finally see the newspaper: "Seattle Man Arrested in Utah" with TED'S MUGSHOT. Picking up mid-argument:
You don't actually believe this garbage, do you? (MORE)
For chrissakes, Liz, I don't know if I should be offended or rolling
on the floor laughing.
It's in all the papers, Ted.
That must make it true then. In six years, have I ever laid a hand on you? When we went shopping for ski racks and that lady got her purse snatched, what did I do? What did I do, Liz?!
You ran him down.
(re: newspaper) Is that the behavior of a man who does this? Jesus, Liz. You wanna search my bags too?
Liz breaks down crying.
(softening) Come here, stupid.
Ted pulls her into a hug.

I didn't tell you because I didn't want you to freak out. It's not exactly something to say over the phone. And, honestly, I was embarrassed. It's all a big misunderstanding. I know it's hard being apart, but we only have four semesters standing between us and our place on the Sound with a Mercedes and a dog, just like we always dreamed.
The moment's interrupted when Molly charges in, excited to see Ted.
Monkey!
I missed you so much!
Ted scoops her up, slings her over his shoulder, and bounces her up and down.
Sack o' potatoes! potatoes!
Molly laughs hysterically.
It's their game.
(to Molly) I missed you so much, monkey. What do you say we get cheeseburgers and root beer floats tonight, and be big fatsoes?
Can we go to the Pepper Pod, Mom? Can we, can we, can we?
Yeah, can we, Mommy?
Liz considers Ted and Molly. A precious sight.
(reassuring, to Liz) It's just a big misunderstanding. I've got it all under control.
Liz finally softens. She believes him.
INT. HAMBURGER STAND - NIGHT
Ted and Liz talk over cheeseburgers and fries while Molly colors with crayons on butcher paper.
You can ask me anything you want.
Did you do it, Ted?
No, monkey. And I hired the best defense attorney in Utah and he says this case has no legs. It's all going to be thrown out.
Why did she pick you out of the lineup?

My lawyer found out the police showed her my picture beforehand -twice. So of course I looked familiar to her. But that's not the worst part. What happened in Utah wasn't dumb luck, Liz. The police already had my name. Someone gave it to them.
(beat) Who would do that to you?
I'm sure I made a few enemies the year I worked the Rockefeller campaign, but that was politics. This is personal. It doesn't add
up, Liz. The traffic stop, sure -that was wrong place, wrong time -but the way it escalated? What the
A sense of paranoia builds as Liz turns to look... until she's startled by-Done!
MOLLY (O.S.)
Molly shows them a CRAYON SHARK DRAWING she just did.
It's a thresher shark. They have a tail as long as their whole body.
They do?! How did you get so smart, monkey?
I read a lot.
Ted tickles Molly, as Liz looks at the DARK SEDAN out the window Ted was referring to. She looks worried for Ted. Over the din of a cocktail reception: ROSS DAVIS (PRE-LAP)
INT. HOTEL BANQUET ROOM - NIGHT
A Republican fundraiser. ROSS DAVIS, Chairman of the WA State Republican Party, slings his arm around Ted, boasting to Liz.
This man single-handedly got Dan Evans re-elected.
Ted secretly rolls his eyes at Liz. steal away with Liz for a moment. He excuses himself to
I know how you hate these things so I won't be completely torn up if you want to leave. I'm fine. Great.
Then you won't mind if I
These people raised money for your defense!
And I'm grateful, but c'mon, I've had yawns more exciting than this.
We do need to get the sitter home by ten, but I thought I was dropping you at the law library to get ready for trial?
Welcome home, stranger.
They're interrupted by a tall woman in thick, tinted glasses, who hugs Ted. Pretty in a boyish way, her name is CAROLE ANN BOONE.
Carole Ann! My goodness. How are you? Have you met my girlfriend Liz? (to Liz) Carole Ann and I worked together at DES in Olympia years ago. (to Carole Ann) How's your son?
He just turned eight.
Oh, that's my daughter's age. (joking) Maybe they'll date.
(excusing himself) Will you excuse us a second, Carole Ann?
Ted walks Liz to the exit.
How will you get to the library?
I just squeezed fifteen grand out of the Republican Party, I'm sure I can hitch a ride.
He pretends to bite her nose. Liz smiles. Completely in love. As she exits, Ted sees her off... then Carole Ann moseys over next to him.
She seems great.
She's the best.
Liz is my rock.
Everything's going to be okay, you know. The young Republican who worked the suicide prevention hotline? How many lives have you saved, for godsakes? (MORE)
Whoever this woman accusing you is, I'm sure she's just mixing you up with another handsome stranger.
There's an awkward pause. Ted smiles bashfully. has a noticeable spark in her eye for Ted.
If you need anything -- anything at all...
Ted blushes, registers the roaring sexual tension.
INT. UW LAW LIBRARY - NIGHT

Ted enters the law library. All the girls turn their heads, one by one, watching him pass. One girl watches him from over her NEWSPAPER. Ted sends them all friendly smiles and winks, loving every second.
INT. BATHROOM / BEDROOM - NIGHT

Liz brushes her teeth before bed, as her eyes land on TED'S SUITCASE. She goes to it, thinks for a beat, then looks through clothes, toiletries, check book, etc. Nothing out of the ordinary. She smells his clothes. Off her ambiguous look...
INT. BOOKSTACKS - UW LAW LIBRARY - NIGHT

Ted browses legal tomes. An ATTRACTIVE GIRL catches his eye through the HORIZONTAL GAP in the bookshelf. They can only see each other's EYES. She smiles at him. Ted smiles back. Moments later, she nudges a book from her side, JUTTING OUT the opposing book on Ted's side. Ted debates, then returns a nudge of his own. They repeat this exchange, a flirtation like PIANO KEYS down the shelf, until they get to the end where there are no more books and they finally see each other's faces. They're both startled by a SECURITY GUARD...
Sir, will you come with me please? ANGLE ON -- the girl reading the newspaper, watching Ted get escorted out, as the camera lands on TED'S MUGSHOT.
EXT. UW LAW LIBRARY - NIGHT
Ted protests as he's escorted out.
I am an alumnus of this school!
Look, buddy, you seem like a nice guy, but you're making the girls
nervous.
What do they think I'm going to do, knock 'em over the head and attack 'em in the bookstacks?
The guard shuts the door on Ted. Fuming, Ted spots a SIMILAR DARK SEDAN across the street. The same one, or different? We can't tell. He marches up to it. Bangs on the window.
Hey! Who are you? following me! I know you're
The car starts up and drives away.
Who sent you?!
As it speeds off into the darkness, Ted fills with anger. Paranoia. Fear. He stands in the quiet night... alone.
INT. LIZ'S BEDROOM - NIGHT

Dead of night. Liz is fast asleep. She stirs, squints her eyes open... and realizes SOMEONE's sitting next to her UNDER THE SHEETS with a LIGHT ON. She screams! Jumps up! Ted startles out from under the sheets. hand. Surprised by Liz's reaction. Flashlight in his
(re: flashlight) What are you doing?
Ted shows her a paperback he's reading. notice it's Capote's In Cold Blood.
Astute viewers may
With a flashlight under the sheets?
I didn't want to wake you.
They realize the absurdity of the situation and break into laughter. As Liz starts to catch her breath...
I think we should get married.
Liz loses her breath completely.
EXT. MOUNTAINS - DAY

Two skiers zzzzZZZZOOOOOOMMMM past in a blur. Ted and Liz cut down a daunting double black diamond. Liz pulls ahead. Ted crouches lower. Gains ground. Neck and neck, fast approaching the finish... ...Liz wins by a hair! She taunts him in celebration. tackles her in the snow and kisses her He
INT. HALLWAY - SALT LAKE CITY COURTHOUSE - DAY
Ted and Liz meet O'Connell outside the courtroom on their way in. Ted's clean shaven in a light blue/gray suit, white shirt, and bow tie -- and noticeably tanner.
Where'd you fly in from, Barbados?
Liz and I hit the slopes yesterday. We're engaged! Once we win this thing, I might just make you my best man.
(re: O'Connell's beard) You'll have to shave for our wedding photos, of course. Won't you scare the jury with that thing?
If we had one. We waived it. Local press all but convicted him so an impartial jury was hard to find.
(to Ted) You waived the jury? Shouldn't we have talked about this?
It's a good thing, Liz.
The whole case hinges on eyewitness testimony which is notoriously unreliable, but the most likely thing for a jury to convict on.
That's why we're putting it all in the hands of the judge.
Hanson's a fair-minded guy with a reputation for controversial acquittals. I went to school with the sonofabitch.
(to O'Connell) You're really going to shave for the wedding? Blow me.
INT. ROOM 310 - SALT LAKE CITY COURTHOUSE - DAY
JUDGE HANSON presides over a high-ceilinged courtroom. jury in the box.
No
Ted finds Liz in the sparse gallery. Sends her a wink as the prosecutor DAVID YOCOM delivers his opening statement.

Your Honor, November 8th, 1974 may have been just another rainy night for most of Murray, Utah, but it was a life-altering tornado of horror for one woman in particular. She was shopping for a birthday
gift at the Fashion Place Shopping Mall when she was approached by a man posing as a police officer. He

As the daughter of a police officer, she felt foolish to question his authority, so she followed him to the parking lot where he led her to what he claimed to be an unmarked police vehicle. When he drove in the opposite direction of the police department, she knew she was in trouble. When he slapped a handcuff around her wrist, she knew she was in danger. Fortunately, before he could latch the other, she twisted free and fled for her life. (beat) Then he pursued her with a crowbar.
The courtroom suddenly drops a few degrees.
INT. ROOM 310 - SALT LAKE CITY COURTHOUSE - LATER
CAROL DARONCH, a soft-featured, 19-year old, strawberry blonde girl, relives the pain of that night.

I was kicking him, and screaming. He held a small black gun to my head and said, "If you don't stop screaming, I'm going to kill you. I'll blow your brains out." I fell backward out of his car, and I ran as fast as I could.
As best you recall, Carol, is that man present in court today? Yes.
Where is he seated?
She timidly looks toward Ted, who looks equally sympathetic and incredulous. Liz sends him support from the gallery.
Let the record show the identification of the defendant. No further questions, Your Honor.
Ted's lawyer JOHN O'CONNELL rises in his usual cowboy hat and boots. He's out for blood.
Ms. DaRonch, it is your testimony now that you did scratch this man?
All my fingernails were broken.
Yet in the preliminary hearing Mr. Yocom asked, "Do you recall ever scratching him with those fingernails?" and you answered, "No." Do you have an explanation
No.
In fact, the officers on November 8th asked if you remembered if you hurt the man in any way, and you told them no then too, didn't you?
I don't remember.
You also said he had a mustache. But shortly after the incident you said he didn't. Then sometime later you decided he did again? Yes.
When did you make that decision?
Right after I decided that he didn't have one.
Ms. DaRonch, had the police shown you any pictures of the defendant prior to your identification of him at the October 2nd lineup? Yes.
You did in fact pick his picture out of a pile. But when asked why you chose his picture specifically, you stated, "It looks something like him, but I really couldn't say for sure."
DaRonch looks down. Tears up.
Tell me, Ms. DaRonch. If you were unsure of your identification then, ten months after the incident, what makes you so sure of it now, nearly a year and a half later? DaRonch is at a complete loss.
Now Ted's on the witness stand -- dapper, relaxed, and confident. Liz sends him encouragement from the gallery.
Do you remember the night of August 16th, 1975?
(smiles) Isn't that why we're all here? A few laughs in the courtroom.
Not even a grin from Yocom.
You were arrested for fleeing an officer.
Objection, Your Honor.
You were arrested for evading an officer. Objection.
